planting.note: Plant at least one male and three female shrubs for berry production; one male can pollinate up to five females. Match the male cultivar's bloom time to the female's — choose male cultivar recommendations from the nursery that match your female selection.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; winterberry is a spectacular native deciduous holly that produces brilliant red (or yellow or orange in some cultivars) berries that encrust every branch after leaf drop in fall, providing dazzling winter interest from October through February when birds finally strip them. It grows naturally in swamps, stream banks, and wet woodland margins and performs best with consistent moisture. This species is dioecious — you must plant both male and female plants, matching cultivars that bloom at the same time, to get berries. Named female cultivars like 'Winter Red', 'Sparkleberry', and 'Red Sprite' (compact) are widely available; match them with the appropriate male. Mulch to maintain soil moisture and acidity.

planting.note: Plant container-grown vines near a sturdy trellis, pergola, or tree trunk in partial to full shade; the vine grows quickly once established but may be slow to emerge in spring.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; pipevine is a vigorous native climbing vine with enormous heart-shaped leaves that creates a dense, lush shade canopy and unusual curved pipe-shaped brown flowers in late spring that lure and temporarily trap small flies as pollinators. It is the essential host plant for the stunning pipevine swallowtail butterfly, which lays eggs exclusively on Aristolochia species — the caterpillars absorb aristolochic acids from the plant, making both larva and adult butterfly toxic to predators. Plant several vines together to provide enough leaf mass for the caterpillars. Train on a sturdy arbor, pergola, or fence; the vine can grow 20–30 feet and covers itself with tropical-looking foliage by midsummer. Cut back hard in late winter to control size.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep and 4–6 inches apart in groups in fall in moist, slightly acidic soil; avoid planting in areas that dry out in summer.

directions: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; atamasco lily is a native zephyr lily of the Southeast, blooming in earliest spring (often around Easter, hence the common name) with large, pristine white funnel-shaped flowers that emerge from wet, grassy meadows, wet pinelands, and moist woodland openings from Virginia to Florida. Unlike most bulbs, it tolerates and actually prefers seasonally wet soil in spring followed by summer moisture (not drought). Plant in a low spot, under deciduous trees, or at a rain garden margin where it will receive extra moisture. The bulbs are toxic. The plant naturalizes readily in Gulf Coast gardens and may bloom sporadically in fall as well after rain events.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in full to partial sun; plants can also be started from divisions in spring for faster bloom.

directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; swamp sunflower is arguably the most spectacular of the native sunflowers for fall bloom, producing hundreds of bright yellow flowers on tall multibranching stems from October through frost — often when almost nothing else is blooming. It grows naturally in wet pine flatwoods, swamp margins, and roadside ditches across the coastal South and is perfectly suited to Gulf Coast zone 8–9 gardens where the late season heat and moisture support its growth. The plant spreads aggressively by rhizome and self-seeding; give it space or divide annually in spring. Monarch butterflies and native bees mob the flowers. Pinch tips twice in early summer for a shorter, more compact plant.

planting.note: Plant container vines near a sturdy support structure in spring; crossvine will attach to brick, wood, or bark by tendrils and does not damage surfaces the way some ivies do. Blooms most heavily in sun but performs well in shade.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moist to moderately dry soil in full sun to full shade; crossvine is a vigorous native semi-evergreen climbing vine of the Southeast with spectacular trumpet-shaped orange-red flowers with yellow throats in early spring that are among the first abundant nectar sources for hummingbirds returning from migration. The common name comes from the cross-shaped pith visible in cross-section of the stem. It climbs by branched tendrils and clings securely to brick, masonry, bark, and fences without causing damage. Unlike many vines, it blooms well even in moderate to deep shade and is one of the best native choices for covering a shaded fence or wall. The foliage is semi-evergreen to evergreen in zone 8–9 and takes on bronze-purple winter color. Prune after bloom to control size.
